description: The figure presents microscale digital image correlation (μ-DIC) maps showing the spatial distribution of local shear strain across the phase boundary between coarse-grained δ-ferrite and ultrafine-grained (UFG) γ+α aggregates in a low-density medium manganese steel (Fe-7.95Mn-0.25C-4.94Al wt.%) subjected to tensile loading along the rolling direction (RD). Panels (a) and (b) display two representative regions captured at increasing macroscopic strain levels of 5%, 10%, 15%, and 20%. The color scale ranges from blue (0% shear strain) to red (30% shear strain), where dashed black lines delineate the phase boundaries and red arrows highlight strain concentration sites. As macroscopic strain increases from 5% to 20%, pronounced strain localization (evident as yellow-to-red regions) develops progressively at the δ-ferrite/γ+α interfaces, indicating severe strain partitioning and incompatibility between the soft δ-ferrite and hard UFG aggregates. These observations provide direct evidence that the iso-strain condition in the RD sample generates high geometrically necessary dislocation (GND) densities and stress concentrations at phase boundaries, which promotes martensitic transformation kinetics.
